source_note: Repo-local starter input. Replace with real upstream fetch logic before launch.

entities.name: North Harbor
entities.summary: North Harbor is the sample high-confidence city. It demonstrates how a detail page can combine score context, supporting metrics, and next-step navigation without becoming a dead end.

faq.question: Why does North Harbor rank near the top in this My Data Site sample?
faq.answer: Its sample score is lifted by strong access and momentum metrics rather than a single extreme outlier.

faq.question: Is the score based on public data or an estimate?
faq.answer: This starter uses sample data only. Real projects should document every source, fallback rule, and estimate boundary.

entities.name: Mesa Grove
entities.summary: Mesa Grove shows how a representative city can have strong momentum with slightly weaker affordability. The detail page should explain those tradeoffs instead of just printing a rank.

faq.question: What makes Mesa Grove different from North Harbor?
faq.answer: Mesa Grove wins more from growth and medium-term upside than from access density.

faq.question: How should My Data Site use these metrics?
faq.answer: Treat them as placeholders for your own source-backed dimensions. Do not ship them as real-world claims.

entities.name: Riverbend
entities.summary: Riverbend is the sample value case. It helps My Data Site demonstrate representative rankings that do not always simply mirror the absolute top score.

faq.question: Why is Riverbend still useful in a city comparison?
faq.answer: Its sample affordability metric is strong enough to make it competitive for value-led comparisons.

faq.question: Should My Data Site separate representative and full rankings?
faq.answer: Yes. Representative rankings are for normal users, while full rankings preserve raw mathematical output.
